reference_answer_points: Services net sales increased in fiscal 2025 versus 2024, primarily from higher advertising, App Store, and cloud services net sales.
Services gross margin was $82.314 billion in 2025 versus $71.050 billion in 2024, and Services gross margin percentage was 75.4% versus 73.9%.
The gross margin improvement was attributed to higher Services net sales and mix, with the percentage gain partly offset by higher costs.
Do not infer total company Services growth rate unless the exact percentage is retrieved from the evidence.
required_caveats: If only the driver paragraph is retrieved without the gross margin table, mark the dollar and percentage values as missing.

common_wrong_conclusions: uses Product gross margin instead of Services
claims unsupported Services revenue dollars or growth rate
synthesis_rubric: cite evidence IDs for each number
separate direction/driver claims from exact table values

query: For Snowflake fiscal 2025, do consumption revenue mechanics, RPO, and customer metrics support good revenue visibility?

target_object_refs.preview: Unlike a subscription-based business model, in which revenue is recognized ratably over the term of the subscription, we generally recognize revenue on consumption.

Customer consumption fluctuates from time to time, and there is a risk that customers will consume our platform at lower levels than we expect, including in response to new software releases or hardware improvements that may make our platform more efficient, a
Unexpected fluctuations in customer consumption may cause actual results to differ from our forecasts.
Because our customers have flexibility in the timing of their consumption, we do not have the visibility into the timing of revenue recognition that a typical subscription-based software company has.

In addition, our platform currently operates on public cloud infrastructure provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure (Azure), and Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and our costs and gross margins are significantly influenced by the prices we are able
We may not have visibility into our future financial position and results of operations.

If we are unable to achieve and sustain profitability, or if we are unable to achieve the revenue growth that we expect from these investments, the value of our business and common stock may significantly decrease.

Our efforts to grow our business have been and may continue to be costlier than we expect, or our revenue growth rate may be slower than we expect, and we may not be able to increase our revenue enough to offset the increase in operating expenses resulting fro
We expect our costs and expenses to increase in future periods.

We will also incur increased general and administrative expenses associated with our growth, including costs related to internal systems, operating as a public company, and targeting regulated industries or markets.

In particular, we intend to continue to invest significant resources to further develop our platform, expand our research and development teams, retain our employees, and acquire other businesses, including in the areas of data science, artificial intelligence
We have a history of operating losses and may not achieve or sustain profitability in the future.

As of January 31, 2025, our remaining performance obligations were approximately $6.9 billion, of which we expect approximately 48% to be recognized as revenue in the twelve months ending January 31, 2026 based on historical customer consumption patterns.
remaining performance obligations | 2025 | $6.9 billion | usd
remaining performance obligations | 2025 | 48% | percent

must_find: 580 customers over $1 million trailing 12-month product revenue

target_object_refs.preview: of Contents
The following tables present a summary of key business metrics, including the most directly comparable financial measure calculated in accordance with GAAP, for the periods presented:
As of January 31, 2025, we had 11,159 total customers, increasing from 9,384 customers as of January 31, 2024.
customers | 2025 | 11,159
customers | 2025 | 9,384

Historical numbers for (i) net revenue retention rate, (ii) customers with trailing 12-month product revenue greater than $1 million, and (iii) Forbes Global 2000 customers reflect any adjustments for acquisitions, consolidations, spin-offs, and other market a
net revenue retention | 2000 | $1 million | usd

As of January 31, 2025, our customers included 745 of the Forbes Global 2000, based on the 2024 Forbes Global 2000 list, and those customers contributed approximately 42% of our revenue for the fiscal year ended January 31, 2025.
revenue | 2025 | 745
revenue | 2025 | 42% | percent
Forbes Global 2000 customers (4) | 2025 | 745 | usd_millions
The number of customers that contributed more than $1 million in trailing 12-month product revenue increased from 455 to 580 as of January 31, 2024 and 2025, respectively.

reference_answer_points: RPO and customer metrics support some visibility: RPO was about $6.9 billion, about 48% expected to be recognized over the next 12 months, with a 2.4-year weighted-average remaining life of capacity contracts.
The customer base broadened to 11,159 total customers, 745 Forbes Global 2000 customers, 126% net revenue retention, and 580 customers with more than $1 million in trailing 12-month product revenue.

The answer must balance this against the risk factor that Snowflake recognizes revenue on consumption, not ratably like a standard subscription model, so timing and amount of revenue recognition depend on future customer consumption and may differ from forecasts.
A comprehensive answer should conclude that visibility is partial, not subscription-like certainty.
required_caveats: If RPO is retrieved without the consumption risk factor, the answer should not overstate revenue predictability.
If customer metrics are missing, mark demand quality/customer expansion as under-evidenced.
common_wrong_conclusions: calls Snowflake a ratable subscription model
treats customer count as sufficient proof of profitability
synthesis_rubric: balance positive visibility indicators against variable consumption
avoid treating RPO as guaranteed next-year revenue
cite both risk and metric evidence

reference_answer_points: Microsoft shows strong cloud growth: Microsoft Cloud revenue rose 23% to $168.9 billion, and Azure and other cloud services revenue grew 34%.

Microsoft also discloses AI infrastructure pressure: gross margin percentage decreased slightly from scaling AI infrastructure, and management expects continued capex for cloud offerings and AI infrastructure/training.
Alphabet's Google Cloud also grew materially: revenue increased to $58.705 billion from $43.229 billion, and operating income rose to $13.910 billion from $6.112 billion.

Alphabet's growth quality looks improved on segment operating income, but cost pressure is still explicit because Google Cloud operating income growth was partly offset by technical infrastructure usage costs and compensation; group capex was $91.4 billion, primarily technical infrastructure.
A good synthesis should avoid declaring either company 'better' without normalizing scale, segment definitions, and cloud margin disclosure differences.
required_caveats: If Microsoft segment margin table is not retrieved, say Microsoft cloud profitability cannot be fully compared to Alphabet's Google Cloud segment profitability.
If Alphabet capex is retrieved but not segment usage-cost language, avoid attributing all capex to Google Cloud alone.
common_wrong_conclusions: attributes all Alphabet capex to Google Cloud
compares Microsoft Cloud revenue directly to Google Cloud without caveat
planner_rubric: GOOGL cloud revenue and operating income
synthesis_rubric: compare growth and cost pressure without forcing a winner
separate company-level capex from segment-level profitability

target_object_refs.preview: The increase in North America operating income in 2025, compared to the prior year, is primarily due to increased unit sales and increased advertising sales, partially offset by increased fulfillment, technology and infrastructure, shipping, and other operatin

reference_answer_points: AWS net sales were $128.725 billion in 2025, up from $107.556 billion in 2024; AWS operating income was $45.606 billion, up from $39.834 billion.

Amazon states the AWS operating income increase was primarily due to higher sales, partially offset by technology infrastructure spending driven by additional investments to support AWS business growth.

The segment note says most infrastructure costs in technology and infrastructure are allocated to AWS based on usage, and technology infrastructure assets are allocated among segments based on usage with the majority allocated to AWS.

For free cash flow, Amazon says FCF is driven by operating income, working-capital efficiency, and cash capital expenditures, and it expects technology and infrastructure spending to increase over time, including for AI/ML initiatives, which can pressure short-term FCF.
A comprehensive answer should present AWS as still highly profitable but capital-intensive.
required_caveats: If only AWS segment revenue is retrieved, mark infrastructure pressure as missing.
If only free-cash-flow overview is retrieved, avoid inventing AWS-specific capex dollars not in the selected evidence.
common_wrong_conclusions: equates AWS operating income growth with FCF growth
misses that infrastructure allocation is usage-based
synthesis_rubric: connect segment profitability to company-level FCF pressure carefully
avoid unsupported AWS capex dollar estimates

query: For NVIDIA fiscal 2025, assess whether Data Center revenue growth looks durable when considering customer concentration, cloud service provider demand, and supply or third-party manufacturing risks.
ideal_facets: customer concentration and indirect customer structure
cloud and consumer internet customer demand
supply capacity commitments and product-transition risk

target_object_refs.preview: Revenue from Data Center computing grew 162% driven primarily by demand for our Hopper computing platform used for large language models, recommendation engines, and generative AI applications.
revenue | data center | 2025 | 162% | percent
Direct Customers
We also have indirect customers, who purchase products through our direct customers; indirect customers include CSPs, consumer internet companies, enterprises, and public sector entities.
Direct Customer C | Jan 26, 2025 | 11 | percent
Direct Customer B | Jan 26, 2025 | 11 | percent
Direct Customer A | Jan 26, 2025 | 12 | percent
No customer represented 10% or more of total revenue for fiscal year 2023.
Direct Customer B | Jan 28, 2024 | 13 | percent
We refer to customers who purchase products directly from NVIDIA as direct customers, such as AIBs, distributors, ODMs, OEMs, and system integrators.
– Sales to direct customers which represented 10% or more of total revenue, all of which were primarily attributable to the Compute & Networking segment, are presented in the following table:
revenue | 2025 | 10% | percent

must_find: industry standard servers from every major cloud provider

target_object_refs.preview: Our customers include the world’s leading public cloud and consumer internet companies, thousands of enterprises and startups, and public sector entities.
We have incurred and may in the future incur inventory provisions or impairments if our inventory or supply or capacity commitments exceed demand for our products or demand declines.

Qualification time for new products, customers anticipating product transitions, and channel partners reducing channel inventory of prior architectures ahead of new product introductions can reduce, or create volatility in, our revenue.

While we have managed prior product transitions and have sold multiple product architectures at the same time, these transitions are difficult, may impair our ability to predict demand and impact our supply mix, and may cause us to incur additional costs.

The increased purchase volumes and integration of new suppliers and contract manufacturers into our supply chain creates more complexity in managing multiple suppliers with variations in production planning, execution and logistics.
With these additions, we have also entered and may continue to enter into prepaid manufacturing and capacity agreements to supply both current and future products.

The increased frequency and complexity of newly introduced products could result in unanticipated quality or production issues that could increase the magnitude of inventory provisions, warranty, or other costs or result in product delays.

The increased frequency of these transitions and the larger number of products and product configurations may magnify the challenges associated with managing our supply and demand which may further create volatility in our revenue.

We have experienced and may in the future experience reduced demand for current generation architectures when customers anticipate transitions, and we may be unable to sell multiple product architectures at the same time for current and future architecture tra
We continue to increase our supply and capacity purchases with existing and new suppliers to support our demand projections and increasing complexity of our data center products.
limited number and geographic concentration of global suppliers, foundries, contract manufacturers, assembly and test providers and memory manufacturers;
failure by our foundries or contract manufacturers to procure raw materials or provide adequate levels of manufacturing or test capacity for our products;
integration of new suppliers and contract manufacturers creating more complexity in managing multiple suppliers with variations in production planning, execution, and logistics;

Dependency on third-party suppliers and their technology to manufacture, assemble, test, or package our products reduces our control over product quantity and quality, manufacturing yields, and product delivery schedules and could harm our business.
We depend on foundries to manufacture our semiconductor wafers using their fabrication equipment and techniques.

delays in product shipments, shortages, a decrease in product quality and/or higher expenses in the event our subcontractors or foundries prioritize our competitors’ or other customers’ orders over ours;

reference_answer_points: The growth evidence is very strong: Data Center computing revenue grew 162%, driven by Hopper for LLMs, recommendation engines, and generative AI; networking grew 51% driven by Ethernet for AI.

Demand context supports durability but is not a guarantee: NVIDIA says its data-center platform is deployed in cloud, hyperscale, on-premises, and edge data centers, and customers include leading public cloud and consumer internet companies.

Customer concentration is a real counterweight: indirect customers include CSPs and consumer internet companies, while direct customers A, B, and C represented 12%, 11%, and 11% of total revenue in fiscal 2025, primarily attributable to Compute & Networking.

Supply-chain risk is material: NVIDIA is increasing supply and capacity purchases, entering prepaid manufacturing/capacity agreements, and faces complexity integrating suppliers and managing product transitions; it also depends on foundries and subcontractors and lacks guaranteed supply of wafers/components/capacity.

A strong final answer should say durability looks supported by AI demand and cloud deployment, but evidence is insufficient for a clean durability conclusion because concentration, supply capacity, product-transition, and third-party manufacturing risks can create revenue/gross-margin volatility.
required_caveats: If direct customer percentage table is missing, customer concentration severity should be marked partial.
If only growth evidence is retrieved, the answer should be considered incomplete because it lacks counter-evidence.
If cloud demand context is retrieved without future backlog/order evidence, avoid claiming guaranteed future demand.
common_wrong_conclusions: answers only with Data Center growth
treats risk-factor boilerplate as impossible-to-use evidence
synthesis_rubric: weigh support and risk evidence explicitly
distinguish durability signal from guaranteed forecast

target_object_refs.preview: Although our subscription contracts are generally non-cancellable, a limited number of customers have the right to cancel their contracts by providing prior written notice to us of their intent to cancel the remainder of the contract term and consumers have a

reference_answer_points: Adobe has strong durability signals: Total Adobe ARR was $25.20 billion exiting fiscal 2025, up 11.5%; RPO was $22.52 billion, up 13%; subscription revenue was $22.904 billion and 96% of total revenue.

Revenue quality is supported by ratable recognition for most subscription revenue and by the disclosure that roughly 65% of RPO, excluding certain enterprise committed-fund agreements, is expected to be recognized over the next 12 months.

The answer must include caveats: RPO excludes some delivered/billed-as-delivered obligations and constrained variable consideration; subscription contracts are generally non-cancellable but some customers have cancellation rights; macro effects can still affect renewals, seat expansion, and new bookings.
A mature answer should call revenue durability strong relative to consumption models, but not risk-free.
required_caveats: If RPO note is missing, next-12-month visibility cannot be assessed.
If only ARR is retrieved, the answer should not infer renewal quality or cash collection details.
If contract caveats are missing, mark durability conclusion as overconfident.
common_wrong_conclusions: misses cancellation or variable consideration caveats
synthesis_rubric: avoid overstating RPO as contracted revenue without exclusions
cite both Item 7 and Item 8 evidence
